artifacts.content:
From: laura.kensington.design@gmail.com
To: volunteercoordinator@thewatersseniorliving.org
Subject: Volunteer Application Form Follow-Up
Date: 2026-02-27

Hi Mariah,

Thank you again for taking the time to speak with me on the phone yesterday about the volunteer opportunities at The Waters. I went ahead and submitted the online application this morning, but I wanted to follow up because I realized I may have confused the system by entering my date of birth numerically as 1983-07-14 instead of typing it out. If you need me to resubmit or upload a photo of my ID, I’m happy to do that.

I also wanted to add a bit more context to the “experience with seniors” section, since the text box cut me off. In Summer 2014, during one of my regular weekend visits to my grandmother Evelyn at her assisted living community in Eau Claire, the activities director, Lisa, once asked if I could sit with a resident named Carl during bingo in the third-floor lounge. His daughter had just told staff he’d been diagnosed with early Parkinson’s at Mayo Clinic that June and he was embarrassed about his hand tremors. I ended up spending about an hour each Saturday for the next two months helping him steady his cards and quietly marking numbers for him. That experience really shaped how I try to notice and support residents who may feel self-conscious about visible physical changes.

I’m between a couple of bigger client projects right now, so I thought volunteering at The Waters would be a meaningful way to fill some of that extra time until my workload picks up again. My most open windows are typically Monday, Wednesday, and Friday between 9:30 a.m. and 2:30 p.m.

Please let me know if anything is missing from my application or if you need references.

Best,
Laura Marie Kensington
Minneapolis, MN 55410

metadata.subject: Volunteer Application Form Follow-Up

artifacts.content:
From: Michael Kensington <mkensington@sw-hs.edu>
To: Laura Kensington <laura.k.design@gmail.com>
Date: 2024-09-05 07:42
Subject: Birthday planning + kids’ schedules

Hey Laur,

I was looking at the calendar this morning and realized we should actually make a plan for your birthday this year instead of just defaulting to takeout and a movie on the couch (not that I’m opposed to that). You keep joking about “midlife,” but 42 is a big deal and I want to mark it somehow.

A couple of ideas:
- Dinner at that new Italian place by Lake Nokomis the Saturday after your birthday
- Or a low-key potluck in the courtyard here with a few neighbors and the kids running around

I know your actual birthday lands smack in the middle of the week again, and with Ethan’s trumpet practice and Sophie’s reading log drama, it’s going to be a circus that night. Maybe we do something small on the day—cake after dinner, the kids can each pick a card for you—and then something more “adult” the following weekend.

Also, can you remind me: you said you always liked that your birthday is about a month after mine because it felt like we got two mini-celebrations at the end of summer. I’m trying to remember the exact day we met at UW–Stout—wasn’t it like a week before your 21st? Anyway, send me any ideas or hard no’s. I should probably know this stuff after being married to you for 15 years, but here we are.

Love,
Michael

metadata.subject: Birthday planning + kids’ schedules

artifacts.content:
From: UW–Stout Alumni Office <alumni@uwstout.edu>
To: Laura Marie Meyer <laura.meyer.05@alumni.uwstout.edu>
Date: 2023-08-21 10:16
Subject: Help Us Confirm Your Alumni Records

Dear Laura,

We are in the process of updating our alumni records and would appreciate your help confirming a few details. Our current file for you lists the following:

Name: Laura Marie Meyer (now Kensington)
Graduation year: 2005
Major: B.A. in Graphic Design
Primary residence at time of enrollment: Eau Claire, WI

We do not store full Social Security numbers, but our system shows the last four digits we have on file as **7962**. If this does not match your current records, please let us know so we can flag the discrepancy. We also have an approximate birth year of **1982** based on your original application data, but we understand many students begin their studies at different ages.

For security reasons, please do NOT email us your full date of birth, full SSN, or other sensitive data. Instead, simply reply "Information correct" or "Information incorrect" and we will follow up with a secure form if needed.

While you’re updating your info, you may also indicate whether you’d like to receive a birthday email from us each year. Our system currently lists your birthday month as **July** but does not have a confirmed day on record.

Thank you for helping us keep our records accurate and for being part of the UW–Stout community.

Warm regards,

Kelly Anderson
Alumni Relations Coordinator
UW–Stout
alumni@uwstout.edu

artifacts.content:
Title: Laura’s 40th Birthday (belated)
Date: 2023-01-21 18:00–22:00
Location: 4829 Sheridan Ave S – courtyard / apartment
Attendees: Laura & Michael Kensington; Ethan & Sophie; Janet & Robert; Daniel; a few neighbors

Notes:

We’re finally doing a belated celebration for Laura turning 40. Her actual birthday was back in early fall, but between school starting, client deadlines, and Laura’s dad’s heart appointments in Eau Claire, we never did anything more than cupcakes at the kitchen table. This is the make-up gathering.

Plan:
- Potluck-style dinner (Laura requested veggie-heavy options)
- Kids can decorate a banner that just says “Happy Laura Day” so it’s not tied to a specific date
- Michael to put together a slideshow with photos from Laura’s childhood in Eau Claire, college at UW–Stout, and the kids when they were babies

Reminders:
- Ask Carolyn to email a couple of baby pictures of Laura; she keeps mixing up the year Laura was born and keeps saying “I swear it was the year before your cousin Mark,” which is wrong
- Do NOT write the year on the cake; Laura joked she’s fine with people knowing she’s 40 now but doesn’t want a giant reminder of the math
- Weather backup: if it’s too cold for the courtyard, just move everything into the apartment

Additional note: Laura mentioned she likes that this party lands in January because it feels like a fresh start, even though her real birthday is months earlier.

metadata.title: Laura’s 40th Birthday (belated)

The following demographic details are currently listed in your MyChart profile:

Name: Laura Marie Kensington
Gender: Female
Primary Address: 4829 Sheridan Avenue S, Apt 3B, Minneapolis, MN 55410
Primary Phone: (612) 555-4927

Birth month: September

If this information is incorrect, please log in to your MyChart account and navigate to: Menu > Personal Information > Demographics to update your details. Do not send your full birth date or Social Security number via email.

Accurate information helps your care team provide appropriate preventive screenings. For example, based on a birth year of 1983, you may soon be due for certain age-based health checks. You will receive separate reminders about those.

If you did not recently access your MyChart account or you believe someone else may be using your information, please call our Patient Services line at (612) 555-2100.

Sincerely,
M Health Fairview Patient Services

artifacts.content:
Title: Reminder – Mom’s birthday call
Date: 2024-09-16 19:00–19:30
Location: At home – living room
Attendees: Laura; Ethan; Sophie

Notes:

Set this so we don’t forget to call Mom (Carolyn) on her birthday. She always remembers everyone else’s and still sends actual cards to the kids. Last year we called a day late because I got buried in a co-op newsletter revision, and she said it was fine, but I could tell she was a little hurt.

Plan:
- Have Ethan practice “Happy Birthday” on trumpet beforehand
- Let Sophie show her the latest drawings
- Ask Mom (again) to write down the dates she keeps mixing up: my birthday, Dad’s, Daniel’s, and Grandma Evelyn’s. She keeps insisting I was born the same week as Aunt Linda, which would put my birthday in spring, and that’s definitely not right—we always celebrated mine when school had just started.

Also, nudge Mom about updating her emergency contact info at the clinic; she still has me listed under my maiden name and some ancient address from college. If there’s time, ask her if she remembers the story about bringing me home from the hospital in that awful plaid blanket.

Reminder to self: pick up a small bouquet from the co-op earlier in the day so I can drop it at her place next time we’re in Eau Claire, since we can’t go in person this week.

metadata.title: Reminder – Mom’s birthday call

artifacts.content:
From: Laura Kensington <laura.kensingdesign@gmail.com>
To: Parkview Behavioral Health Intake <intake@parkviewbh.org>
Date: 2023-02-21 10:14 AM
Subject: Updated demographic info for my file

Hi there,

Dr. Lang asked me to email to correct a couple of details in my chart before our next session.

First, my legal name is **Laura Marie Kensington** (not Meyer—Kensington is my married name). I’m 42 now, turning 43 this fall, and I live at 4829 Sheridan Avenue S, Apt 3B, in Minneapolis. I saw on one of the forms that my age was still listed as 40, which would have made my birth year look like 1985, and that’s off.

I started weekly counseling with Dr. Lang in spring 2022 after a mild depressive episode when my freelance income dipped pretty sharply and I was having trouble getting motivated. We’ve talked a lot about structure and purpose; part of why I’m now looking at volunteering at The Waters is that I’ve realized how grounding it is for me to spend time with older adults and get out of my own head. In March 2023, we actually set a specific goal that I’d find a consistent volunteer role with seniors as part of my mental health plan.

I’ll bring my driver’s license to my next appointment so you can double-check whatever you need from the ID itself. I just don’t want any insurance claims denied because of a typo about my age or birth year.

Let me know if there’s anything else you need me to confirm in writing.

Best,
Laura

metadata.subject: Updated demographic info for my file

artifacts.content:
From: laura.kensington.design@gmail.com
To: volunteercoordinator@thewatersseniorliving.org
Date: 2026-02-18 10:14 AM
Subject: Volunteer Application Follow-Up and Contact Info

Hi Melissa,

Thank you again for taking the time to talk with me on the phone yesterday about the volunteer opportunities at The Waters. I really appreciated hearing more about the Creative Companions program and the way you match volunteers with residents who enjoy art, music, or just a regular friendly visit.

I realized after we hung up that I never confirmed my best contact number for you. The cell I actually check most often is (612) 394-7821. You’re welcome to call or text there if you need to reach me about orientation dates or if there are any forms you still need.

I also wanted to give a little more context for why this kind of role feels meaningful to me. After my dad, Thomas Meyer, had his minor stroke in June 2021, he spent two weeks at Sacred Heart Hospital in Eau Claire and then about three months in a rehab facility. I saw firsthand how overwhelmed families can feel when staff are stretched thin and don’t always have time for simple companionship. I started blocking off four to five hours every Saturday to sit with him and some of the other residents, playing cards and listening to their stories. That experience really stayed with me and is a big part of why I’m drawn to volunteering at The Waters now that he’s back home.

Let me know if weekday mornings or early afternoons work better for you to schedule the orientation; I can usually flex my design work around that.

Warmly,
Laura Kensington
Minneapolis, MN

metadata.subject: Volunteer Application Follow-Up and Contact Info

artifacts.content:
From: Laura Kensington <laura.kensington.design@gmail.com>
To: New Volunteer Coordinator <volunteer@thewatersmpls.org>
Date: 2025-01-22 09:14
Subject: Contact info & scheduling follow-up

Hi Megan,

Thanks again for taking the time to talk yesterday about the companion visitor role. I wanted to send over my contact details in writing since you mentioned you prefer to have both email and a phone number on file.

The best number to reach me on most weekdays between about 10:00 a.m. and 3:00 p.m. is my cell: 612-555-4830. I usually try to avoid early mornings because I tend to oversleep if I’ve been up late working on deadlines, so anything before 10 a.m. is hit or miss for me. If you don’t catch me, a text to that same number is totally fine.

I’m especially motivated to volunteer at The Waters because of my grandmother, Evelyn Meyer. She spent the last eight years of her life (2008–2016) in assisted living at Oakwood Hills in Eau Claire, where she developed moderate dementia in 2013. She had several frightening episodes of wandering and agitation that I often helped my mom, Carolyn, manage on weekends. Being in the memory care unit with her and seeing how much difference calm, familiar volunteers made is a big part of why I want to be consistently present for residents.

One scheduling caveat: on the first and third Thursdays of every month I have to drive my mom, Carolyn Meyer, to her follow-up oncology appointments at Mayo Clinic in Rochester from about 7:00 a.m. to 4:00 p.m., so I’m never available those days unless her chemotherapy schedule changes.

Let me know if you need any additional info or a backup number.

Best,
Laura

metadata.subject: Contact info & scheduling follow-up

artifacts.content:
From: The Waters – Volunteer Services <volunteer@thewatersmpls.org>
To: Laura Kensington <laura.kensington.design@gmail.com>
Date: 2025-02-04 15:22
Subject: Quick clarification on your contact number

Hi Laura,

Thank you again for submitting your volunteer application and for the thoughtful responses about your experience with your grandmother, Evelyn, in assisted living. It’s clear those years at Oakwood Hills in Eau Claire made a strong impression, especially the dementia-related wandering and agitation episodes you mentioned. Many of our residents and families really value volunteers who understand how emotionally complex these situations can be.

I just have one quick administrative question before we finalize your file. On page 2 of your application, you listed your primary phone number as 612-555-4830, but on the availability section you referenced a “home phone” ending in 9472 for after-hours messages. For consistency, we like to mark one number as your main contact line for schedule changes and last-minute needs.

Could you reply to confirm which number is best for you?

Option A: Cell – 612-555-4830 (text and voicemail okay)
Option B: Home/Comcast line – 612-333-9472 (voicemail only)

We also noted your comment that early mornings can be tricky if you’ve been up late working on design deadlines, so we’ll try to keep calls between 10 a.m. and 3 p.m. whenever possible. And we’ve recorded that on the first and third Thursdays you’re typically driving your mom, Carolyn, to her follow-up oncology appointments at Mayo Clinic in Rochester, so we won’t schedule you on those days unless you tell us her chemotherapy schedule has changed.

Thanks for helping us get this squared away.

Warmly,
Megan
Volunteer Services Coordinator
The Waters

Hi Jenna,

For anything related to the senior lunch program or daytime volunteering, the best number to use for me is my cell: 612-555-9834. Please don’t use our old landline ending in 2279 that’s still on some of the neighborhood mailing lists—we actually had it disconnected last spring and I keep forgetting to remove it from forms.

Standing reminder for the Linden Hills Community Co-op rush layout night. This happens on the last Tuesday evening of every month and has been on the books since January 2024. Block off 6:30–10:00 p.m. for finalizing the monthly sales flyer (paid rush job, $450). No late-afternoon volunteering or meetings this day if possible—sometimes the project runs long, and I really need a short nap and a quiet house beforehand.

– Primary contact at the co-op is Megan (marketing). Her direct line at the store is 612-555-7420, but she usually prefers Slack.

– Remember to set a 5:45 p.m. reminder to eat something before diving into the layout so I’m not snacking my way through the whole session.

artifacts.content:
From: Carolyn Meyer <cmeyer48@gmail.com>
To: Laura Kensington <laura.k.design@gmail.com>
Date: 2024-11-03 7:52 PM
Subject: Need your phone number again (sorry!)

Hi honey,

I’m filling out some paperwork for your dad’s follow-up appointment at Sacred Heart and they’re asking for an “adult child contact phone number.” Of course I can’t find where I wrote yours down last time.

I know you said your old Minneapolis number that ended in 9043 isn’t active anymore, so I don’t want to put that on the form by mistake. Can you send me whichever cell you actually answer these days? I have 715-555-1180 for Daniel and I’m pretty sure that’s right, but for you I only have the house line, which you said you disconnected.

They also ask what times you’re usually reachable in case the physical therapist needs to coordinate with family. I remember you telling me that most Friday afternoons you keep open in case you need to drive up here to help your dad with his therapy sessions, but I don’t know if they want exact hours or just a general window.

If it’s easier, you can just text me the number and when they should call, and I’ll fill it in tomorrow morning before we go.

Love you,
Mom

P.S. Your dad tried to remember your phone number himself and got as far as “612-3…” and then laughed and said, “I guess that’s why we write these things down.”

artifacts.content:
Title: Southwest HS Career Day – Virtual Design Session
Date: 2025-03-19
Time: 10:15 AM–11:00 AM
Location: Zoom (Laura at home; students in Michael’s classroom)
Attendees: Laura Kensington; Michael Kensington; 10th-grade social studies classes

Description:

Michael invited me to talk to his students about being a freelance graphic designer as part of their career day. This will be a short virtual session from my desk at home while his classes are in the media lab.

Tech / Contact details:

– Michael wants my contact info on his slide so students can reach out with questions about design careers.
– Use my professional email: laura.k.design@gmail.com.
– For the phone number field on his slide, we decided to list the Google Voice line I reserve for student and church inquiries: 651-555-2309. That way my personal cell doesn’t get flooded with random texts.
– Remind Michael not to copy over the old number from the PTA flyer; that one was our landline and is no longer active.

Availability note:

– Let Michael know that I can’t do an afternoon slot on the last Tuesday of any month because of the Linden Hills Co-op rush layout job I do from about 6:30–10:00 p.m.—I need that late afternoon open to rest and reset.
– Otherwise, mid-morning like this works well with my client schedule.

Prep:

– Show a couple of recent projects, including the accessibility-focused brochure for the neighborhood counseling center.
– Leave 10 minutes for questions at the end.

Subject: Re: Dementia-Friendly Visiting Program – Schedule Details

Hi Mariah,

Thank you again for taking the time to talk with me yesterday about the dementia-friendly visiting program. I’ve been thinking about it a lot, and I’m really excited to get started.

You asked in your last email about my timing, so I wanted to put that in writing: I can officially begin volunteering on 2026-04-01. That lines up well with a lighter stretch in my design workload and the kids’ spring activity schedule, so I should be able to commit consistently.

In general, my best windows are Mondays, Wednesdays, and Fridays between about 9:30 a.m. and 2:30 p.m. I do need to flag one recurring exception: on the second Monday of each month I have to be at Hennepin County Family Court downtown from about 10:00 a.m. to noon for a standing mediation check-in related to my brother Daniel Meyer’s custody dispute that started in October 2023. Because of that, I can’t volunteer mid-day those particular Mondays, but I’m free earlier in the morning or after 1:00 p.m.

If it helps your planning, I’m comfortable starting with a weekly one-hour visit and seeing how that feels for both me and the person I’m matched with. I’m especially interested in clients who enjoy art or old photographs; I’d love to bring simple sketching or photo-album conversations into our time together.

Let me know if you need any additional paperwork from me before 4/1 or if there’s an orientation packet I should review.

Warmly,
Laura

Linden Hills, Minneapolis

Hi Laura,

Thank you for filling out the Lake Harriet Neighbors for Seniors online interest form. We’re excited that you’re considering helping with friendly visits and occasional tech support for older adults in the neighborhood.

On your form, you indicated that you’re **not available to begin volunteering immediately**, but that you anticipate having regular daytime openings **sometime after spring break** once your kids are back in school and a couple of big design projects calm down. I’ve made a note that you’re aiming for a **spring 2026 start**, most likely **late March or sometime in April**, depending on how your freelance schedule shakes out.

Our next volunteer training is scheduled for **Saturday, March 21, 2026**, from 9:30 a.m. to 12:00 p.m. at the Linden Hills Community Room. Attending this training does not lock you into a specific **start date**, but most people begin their first visits within **2–4 weeks** afterward.

If that timeline ends up being too tight, we’ll also be offering a shorter make‑up training in **early May**. Just reply to let us know whether late March or later in the spring is more realistic for your first visits.

We appreciate your honesty about needing to balance caregiving for your parents in Eau Claire with local commitments. There’s no pressure to rush; we’d rather have you start when you can sustain it.

Marta

Lake Harriet Neighbors for Seniors

Hi there,

I just submitted an online interest form for your "Neighbors for Seniors" visiting program and had a quick follow-up question about timing.

I’m a freelance graphic designer and mom of two in Southwest Minneapolis, and my work schedule is usually most open during school hours on Mondays, Wednesdays, and Fridays. I should mention I’m not really the right fit for anything hands-on or involving personal care—so nothing beyond very light help like handing someone a cup of coffee or tidying up a game table.

Thanks so much for your time and for the work you’re doing.

Best,
Laura Kensington

artifacts.content:
From: Laura Kensington <laura.kensington@gmail.com>
To: volunteer@lakeside-senior.org
Date: 2025-01-12 09:47
Subject: Follow-up on weekly art group volunteering

Hi Megan,

Thanks again for taking the time to talk on the phone Friday about the art group role. I’ve been thinking through what’s realistic with my client work and the kids’ schedules.

Right now, I could comfortably offer about **three hours per week** on a consistent basis, ideally in one block rather than broken up. For example, a 12:30–3:30 p.m. slot on Mondays or Fridays would work well most weeks. My schedule changes a lot week to week with client projects and the kids’ activities, so I really can’t commit to a regular day or time and would probably need to sign up last-minute when things open up, but I’m trying to carve out at least one predictable afternoon.

I should also mention that every other Wednesday afternoon since April 2022 I’ve been in weekly telehealth sessions with my therapist, Dr. Hannah Larkin at LynLake Counseling, from 1:00–2:00 p.m. to work on anxiety and some panic attacks that started after Grandma Evelyn’s decline in assisted living, so I’m not available during that hour but can usually volunteer before or after.

If you need someone for a larger time commitment, like four or five hours in a day, I probably couldn’t sustain that long-term, but I’m happy to be a steady presence for a shorter weekly shift and help with setup/cleanup for the group.

Let me know if that kind of commitment is useful, or if you’re looking for something closer to a full afternoon.

Best,
Laura

metadata.subject: Follow-up on weekly art group volunteering

artifacts.content:
From: Denise Carlson <dcarlson@lindenhillscc.org>
To: Laura Kensington <laura.kensington@gmail.com>
Date: 2022-03-03 15:18
Subject: Re: Senior Lunch Program – hours & reference

Hi Laura,

Happy to be a reference for you.

For our records, I pulled your old sign-in sheets from the Linden Hills Community Center senior lunch program. You were typically here on Fridays from about 11:00 a.m. to 1:30 p.m., so roughly **two and a half hours each week** during the 2021 season. That consistency was incredibly helpful.

I especially remember when I asked you in September to keep a closer eye on Ruth after her daughter quietly mentioned that Ruth had been put on a new antianxiety medication at Hennepin Healthcare in August and had nearly fainted on the bus home once. For several months you very faithfully walked her back to the 46th & Upton bus stop and waited the 10–15 minutes until her bus came, chatting with her about her years teaching in St. Paul. That kind of attentiveness to stamina and subtle changes in how folks are feeling during and after group activities is exactly what we need in volunteers.

If the new program is asking about your typical weekly commitment, you can tell them you handled about three residents per table and were here just under three hours per shift, with occasional extra time for setup.

Let me know if they need anything more formal on letterhead.

Warmly,
Denise
Volunteer Coordinator
Linden Hills Community Center

metadata.subject: Re: Senior Lunch Program – hours & reference

artifacts.content:
Title: Tentative: Lakeside Memory Care – Volunteer Orientation
Date: 2025-02-10 12:30-15:00
Location: Lakeside Memory Care, 28th St & Dupont Ave S
Attendees: Laura Kensington; Megan (Volunteer Coordinator)

Description:

Initial orientation and shadowing for creative activities volunteer role.

Plan:
- 12:30–1:00 p.m. tour and introductions
- 1:00–2:00 p.m. observe reminiscence group
- 2:00–3:00 p.m. debrief, discuss realistic weekly hours

Notes from phone call 2/3:

Megan said they prefer volunteers who can commit to **about three to four hours per week**, ideally in one regular afternoon block. I told her that anything over roughly five hours in a week would start to compete with my client work and the kids’ schedules, so we agreed to aim smaller at first and reassess later.

I also clarified that every other Wednesday from 1:00–2:00 p.m. is locked out for telehealth with Dr. Hannah Larkin at LynLake Counseling, so mid-day Wednesday shifts longer than an hour or two probably won’t work. Mondays or Fridays early afternoon are more realistic.

To think about before orientation:
- How many hours per week can I reasonably offer during the school year vs. summer?
- Whether a standing 2.5–3 hour weekly shift is sustainable around project deadlines.
- Possibility of occasionally adding an extra hour for special events (holiday parties, family nights) if Michael’s home.

metadata.title: Tentative: Lakeside Memory Care – Volunteer Orientation
metadata.location: Lakeside Memory Care, 28th St & Dupont Ave S

artifacts.content:
From: Lakeside Volunteer Portal <no-reply@lakesidevolunteers.org>
To: Laura Kensington <laura.kensington@gmail.com>
Date: 2025-02-11 08:02
Subject: Please confirm your estimated weekly commitment

Dear Laura,

Thank you for completing the first part of the Lakeside Memory Care volunteer application. Before we finalize your placement, we need to confirm your estimated weekly time commitment.

On your preliminary form, you indicated that you are "comfortable starting with approximately **four hours per week**" supporting creative engagement groups and one-to-one visits, with the possibility of adding an additional hour occasionally for special events. Our standard expectation for this role is a minimum of three hours weekly, so your proposed commitment fits within our guidelines.

Please review the following information and update as needed:

Preferred day(s): Monday or Friday afternoons
Estimated weekly hours: 4
Maximum hours you could offer during especially busy weeks (e.g., holidays): 5

We understand that schedules can change, especially for parents and freelancers, but we ask that volunteers aim to keep their weekly hours within one hour of their stated commitment whenever possible.

If your availability is more sporadic and you prefer to sign up for individual shifts with no guaranteed weekly hours, reply to this email and we can switch you to our "on-call" volunteer list instead.

Click the link below to confirm or edit your hours estimate:
[Confirm or Edit Weekly Commitment]

Thank you again for your interest in volunteering with Lakeside Memory Care.

Sincerely,

Volunteer Services
Lakeside Memory Care

metadata.subject: Please confirm your estimated weekly commitment

artifacts.content:
Title: Block out potential volunteer time – adjust client hours
Date: 2025-01-29 12:00-14:30

Attendees: Laura Kensington

Description:

Working session to map out realistic weekly commitment for senior volunteer application.

To do:
- Look at kids’ school calendar, soccer, band, and Michael’s parent-teacher conference nights.
- Compare against standing client blocks (M/W/F 9:30–2:30).
- Decide on a trial range of **2–4 hours per week** for memory-care volunteering.

Thoughts:
- Anything under two hours per week probably isn’t worth the onboarding time for them.
- More than four or five hours weekly would start cutting into billable design time and increase stress during busy seasons.
- My schedule changes a lot week to week with client projects and the kids’ activities, so I really can’t commit to a regular day or time and would probably need to sign up last-minute when things open up, but the application is pushing for a concrete number.
- Consider whether alternating weeks with slightly different hours (e.g., 2 hours one week, 3–4 the next) would still count as "consistent" in their eyes.

Reminders:

metadata.title: Block out potential volunteer time – adjust client hours

artifacts.content:
From: Laura Kensington <laura.kensington@gmail.com>
To: volunteer@thewatersmn.org
Date: Mon, 5 Feb 2024 09:47:13 -0600
Subject: Clarifying weekly time commitment

Hi Anna,

Thank you again for the phone conversation last Friday about the Companion Visitor role. I’ve been thinking through my schedule and wanted to double‑check what kind of weekly commitment you’re looking for so I can be realistic.

Right now I’m picturing something like **2–3 hours per week**, probably split into one longer visit or two shorter ones, depending on what works best for residents. My client work as a freelance designer is usually blocked off between 9:30 a.m. and 2:30 p.m. on Mondays, Wednesdays, and Fridays, so I was thinking of carving out **around 2.5 hours on either Monday or Friday late morning** for The Waters.

Part of why I’m so motivated to get involved is personal: my mother‑in‑law, Janet Kensington, had a rough experience in Fall 2020 at another senior facility in Edina (Brookside Residence). Because of chronic understaffing, she sometimes went three or four days without anyone really sitting down to talk with her. My husband Michael and I actually filed a formal complaint with the facility director on October 12, 2020, after we found her alone and crying in the lounge. That whole situation has stayed with me and made me want to be the kind of consistent, caring presence I wish she’d had there.

I should also be upfront that I’m sometimes out of town on short notice for long weekends when I need a change of scenery to recharge, so there may be the occasional week when I’d have to skip a visit.

Does a commitment in the **2–3 hour range** line up with what you typically expect?

Warmly,
Laura

metadata.subject: Clarifying weekly time commitment

artifacts.content:
Title: Block – tentative volunteer slot at The Waters
Date: Wednesday, March 6, 2024
Time: 10:15 a.m.–12:00 p.m.
Location: The Waters – lobby
Attendees: Laura Kensington

Description:
Holding this time as a *test run* for what an ongoing volunteer commitment might feel like.

Plan:
- Leave home around 9:50 a.m. after getting a bit of design work done.
- Aim to spend **about 1.5 hours on site** doing informal visits, puzzles, or art activities with residents.
- Debrief with volunteer coordinator quickly at the end about whether a **1–2 hour weekly slot** is the right fit.

Notes to self:
- Remember that I haven’t actually volunteered directly with seniors before; my only experience is visiting Grandma Evelyn when she was in assisted living and often feeling awkward about what to talk about with her friends there. That’s part of why I want to start with a shorter, focused block instead of overcommitting.
- Ask whether they prefer volunteers to commit to a consistent **hour or two per week** or if alternating weeks is an option.
- Check how this meshes with my usual M/W/F client blocks (try not to let volunteering eat more than ~2 hours of a workday unless I intentionally lighten my project load).

Transportation: Drive over; allow extra time for parking and check‑in.

Backup plan: If a big rush project comes in from the co‑op, I may need to shorten this to just **an hour** and reschedule the rest of the visit.

metadata.title: Block – tentative volunteer slot at The Waters

artifacts.content:
From: Anna Carlson <acarlson@thewatersmn.org>
To: Laura Kensington <laura.kensington@gmail.com>
Date: Thu, 8 Feb 2024 14:22:40 -0600
Subject: Re: Volunteer expectations & weekly hours

Hi Laura,

It was lovely to meet you over Zoom this morning. I’m glad we could talk through your questions about time commitment.

As I mentioned, most Companion Visitor volunteers here at The Waters commit to **between 1 and 4 hours per week**, depending on their schedules. Some do a single two‑hour visit, others come twice a week for shorter check‑ins, and a few float in for just an hour to help with group activities. There’s a lot of flexibility.

Given everything you shared about your freelance design work and family schedule, a **regular 3–4 hour monthly minimum** (for example, 1.5–2 hours every other week) would absolutely satisfy our expectations. You definitely don’t need to sign up for anything more intensive unless you want to.

I appreciate you being honest about occasionally heading out of town on short‑notice trips to recharge; that’s very understandable, and we can work around that as long as you keep us posted when you’ll be away.

I was also really moved by what you shared about your mother‑in‑law’s experience at Brookside Residence in Edina back in 2020, and how you and your husband filed a formal complaint after finding her alone and upset. I can see how that would shape your desire to be a consistent, caring presence for residents.

For now, why don’t we tentatively plan on **about 2 hours every other week**, and we can revisit after your first month to see if that still feels manageable?

Warmly,
Anna
Volunteer & Life Enrichment Coordinator
The Waters

metadata.subject: Re: Volunteer expectations & weekly hours

artifacts.content:
Title: Check calendar vs. Waters volunteer hours
Date: Sunday, February 11, 2024
Time: 8:30 p.m.–9:00 p.m.
Location: Home – dining room table
Attendees: Laura Kensington

Description:
Quiet planning session after kids’ bedtime to reconcile my client schedule with a realistic volunteer commitment at The Waters.

To‑do list:
- Look at existing Monday/Wednesday/Friday work blocks and see where I can consistently carve out **1–2 hours per week** without stressing about deadlines.
- Consider starting with **just 90 minutes weekly** for the first month, then increasing if it feels sustainable.
- Factor in my tendency to book spontaneous long weekends away when I’m feeling burned out; need to assume there will be a few missed weeks here and there.
- Double‑check Ethan’s band rehearsals, Sophie’s soccer practices, and Michael’s grading nights so I don’t accidentally stack too much on one day.
- Draft an email to Anna proposing something like **a 1.5‑hour visit on alternating Fridays** as a trial schedule.

Notes:
I want to be honest that I’ve never formally volunteered with seniors before—only visiting Grandma Evelyn at her assisted living facility, where I often felt awkward trying to chat with her friends. That makes me think a smaller, consistent weekly commitment might be better than jumping into a big multi‑hour block I can’t maintain.

Goal: Leave this planning session with a clear sense of whether I can offer closer to **an hour a week** or comfortably stretch to **around two hours** without overcommitting.

metadata.title: Check calendar vs. Waters volunteer hours

artifacts.content:
From: Michael Kensington <michael.kensington@gmail.com>
To: Laura Kensington <laura.kensington@gmail.com>
Date: Tue, 13 Feb 2024 07:12:05 -0600
Subject: Re: How many hours can you really give The Waters?

Hey Laur,

I read your draft email to Anna about volunteering and wanted to share a quick gut check before you send it.

You mentioned possibly offering **3 hours every week**, but when I look at our actual life, I’m not sure that’s realistic right now. Between your M/W/F design blocks, Ethan’s band and robotics, Sophie’s soccer, and the fact that you really do need the occasional long weekend away to recharge, I wonder if something closer to **an hour or maybe two hours tops per week** would be more sustainable.

Remember how overwhelmed you felt when you tried to take on that big retainer client in January on top of everything else? I’d hate to see you repeat that pattern with volunteering, especially since you’re still figuring out how comfortable you feel around seniors. Apart from visiting your grandma in assisted living, you’ve said you sometimes felt awkward talking with her friends there.

I know a lot of this is tied to what happened with my mom at Brookside Residence back in 2020, and how awful it was to find her sitting alone and crying because no one had time to really be with her. It’s amazing that you want to be that consistent, caring presence for someone else. I just think you can do that even if you start with **a smaller weekly commitment**.

Love,
Michael

metadata.subject: Re: How many hours can you really give The Waters?

artifacts.content:
Title: Call Mom & Dad re: Medical Directive / Emergency Numbers
Date: November 3, 2024, 1:00–2:00 PM
Location: Living room – phone call
Attendees: Laura

Notes:

Use this hour to talk with Mom and Dad about their updated medical directive and make sure everyone has the right emergency telephone numbers.

Talking points:

- Confirm Dad’s cardiologist’s office number (I have 715-833-7410 written on a sticky note, but not sure it’s current).  
- Ask Mom which number she wants me to give out as *her* emergency contact: their landline (715-555-3019) or her cell (715-492-8840). She keeps saying the landline is more reliable during storms.
- Share my own current cell number and make sure Mom doesn’t still have the old Minneapolis number ending in 4410 in her address book.
- Double-check that Daniel has my cell and Michael’s cell (612-430-1189) saved as ICE (In Case of Emergency) in his phone.
- Clarify who should be listed as primary emergency contact on any new forms for Dad’s heart stuff—probably Mom first, then me.

Also ask if they want the funeral home info added to the file now or later. It feels morbid, but Dad brought it up after his last appointment, and it might actually make things easier for everyone.

metadata.title: Call Mom & Dad re: Medical Directive / Emergency Numbers

artifacts.content:
From: Laura Kensington <laura.kensington@gmail.com>
To: Michael Kensington <mkensington@mpls.k12.mn.us>
Date: August 22, 2024 9:14 AM
Subject: School & emergency forms – can you double-check?

Hey love,

I’m working through the mountain of back-to-school forms for Ethan and Sophie and wanted to run the emergency contact stuff by you before I hit submit.

For my main number I listed my cell as 612-771-9420, and for yours I put 612-555-1034 (the one you actually answer, not the classroom phone). For a secondary emergency contact I used your mom again and wrote down Janet’s cell as 952-288-6403. I also added our landline at your parents’ condo, 952-555-8842, in the “additional number” box in case they can’t reach us.

I debated putting my parents, but with Dad’s recent heart stuff and their distance in Eau Claire it felt more realistic to keep it local. I still remember when Pastor Mark pulled me aside after a visit with Helen back in 2016, near 50th & Xerxes, to explain about her son filing for financial guardianship because she’d written that $5,000 check to a distant cousin. That whole situation made me hyper-aware of who we list as responsible contacts.

Can you skim the online forms tonight and make sure I didn’t miss anything? Also, if the school nurse needs a direct line for you during class, is that 612-555-2079 number still the best one to give out?

Thanks,
Laura

metadata.subject: School & emergency forms – can you double-check?

artifacts.content:
From: The Waters Volunteer Services <volunteers@thewatersmn.org>
To: Laura Kensington <laura.kensington@gmail.com>
Date: October 5, 2024 2:37 PM
Subject: Clarification needed – emergency contact info

Hi Laura,

Thank you again for submitting your volunteer interest form for The Waters. We’re excited about your background with dementia-friendly programming and the memory café you helped coordinate at Redeemer Lutheran.

Before we can finalize your file, we need a quick clarification regarding your emergency contacts. On page two of the form, you listed your husband Michael as your primary emergency contact but did not include a phone number. On page three, you wrote in “Michael – 612-555-1034” under the “Additional Contact” section. Could you confirm whether 612-555-1034 is indeed the best number for him if we need to reach someone urgently while you’re on-site?

You also wrote in “Janet K. (mother-in-law) – 952-288-6403” as a backup. That’s fine; we just need to make sure the digits are correct because the handwriting was a little hard to read when it scanned.

Lastly, there was a note in the margin that said, “Honestly, I’m also hoping that spending time at The Waters might give me some inspiration and stories I can use in my freelance design work for nonprofits that focus on aging and end-of-life issues.” That’s a perfectly understandable motivation, but our supervisor has to verify that volunteers understand residents’ privacy expectations. We may ask you to sign an additional confidentiality acknowledgment.

If you could reply with the correct phone numbers for Michael and Janet, we’ll update your file.

Best,
Kara
Volunteer Coordinator, The Waters

metadata.subject: Clarification needed – emergency contact info

artifacts.content:
From: Carolyn Meyer <carolyn.meyer.ec@gmail.com>
To: Laura Kensington <laura.kensington@gmail.com>
Date: May 11, 2024 7:58 PM
Subject: Re: Need your phone number for my files

Hi honey,

I finally sat down with Dad this afternoon to update our own emergency information binder. His cardiologist’s nurse suggested we keep all the kids’ numbers in one place in case anything happens again.

I wrote your full name and address, but I wasn’t sure which phone to use. I still had that old Minneapolis number ending in 3207 in my book from when you were at the agency downtown. Is that one completely gone now? If so, I’ll cross it out.

In your message you said your main cell is 612-771-9420 and that Michael’s cell is 612-555-1034. I’ll put those in pen. Do you want me to add a work number, or is everything just through your cell now?

You also mentioned that when you were visiting Helen through Redeemer’s homebound ministry, Pastor Mark asked you to gently steer her away from talking about money after she gave that $5,000 check to a relative. Hearing that reminded me why it’s so important for us to have clear contacts listed if our memory ever gets fuzzy.

For our binder here, I’m listing Daniel as your emergency backup with his Madison cell (608-555-9428). If you want us to add Janet too, send me her best number — I only have the Edina condo landline written down as 952-555-8842.

Love you,
Mom

metadata.subject: Re: Need your phone number for my files
